quotes.verbatim_quote: Needless to say, using different rhythms in every bar is more sophisticated but it is not necessarily more effective. This exercise improves your awareness of which rhythms you are using as well as your awareness of where you are in the bar. More notes per bar is not better than a few, just different.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the method's foundational rule about note density: sophistication and effectiveness are not the same, and the goal is awareness rather than complexity.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The next step is to try playing a specific number of notes but this time play them independent of
bar lines. In other words, pick a specific number of notes and then play that grouping as many or
i . . . . ‘ :

few times as you like within a bar, over the bar, or through the bar. The following example uses
three-note groupings within and over the bar lines.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the core technique of the chapter: treating note groupings as independent of bar-line structure, which is the method's characteristic move toward polyrhythmic thinking.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Another idea to consider is that your note groupings don’t necessarily have to be connected. In this
following example I am thinking of three note groupings although the listener wouldn’t be able to
tell that because the groupings are overlapped. The point being made here is that practicing in this
way can lead you to new or different rhythmic ideas. How I’m thinking about these groupings of
notes will effect the articulation of the lines.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This rule that internal mental grouping affects articulation even when invisible to the listener is a core pedagogical principle linking thought process to physical execution.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
As you can see this exercise can be as exoteric or esoteric as you like depending on your own imag-
ination. It creates rhythms that you might not ordinarily play and it also serves as a useful tool to
begin hearing polyrhythms. For example, take a five-note grouping, they could be played as simply
as this:
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage names the ultimate conceptual destination of the grouping exercises and positions the five-note grouping as the entry point.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The five notes can be played as 1 beat or 1 1/2 beats. 2 beats, 3 beats, or + beats, etc.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives the explicit rule for rhythmic flexibility of a single note grouping, showing that the same five notes can occupy any duration.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This chapter contains 22 one-bar rhythms as well as a new tune to work with. Try playing each I

one-bar rhythm through Tune 2. Play each rhythm and feel it as if you were a rhythm instrument,
feel the rhythm inside your body. Play through each of the 22 rhythms and get a feel for very I
one. Of course, add to the list! The chart is limited to primarily quarter notes and eighth notes. Six-

teenth notes and triplets have been purposely omitted at this point.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines scope and embodied practice instruction for the 22-rhythm exercise, and the deliberate omission of sixteenth notes/triplets marks a pedagogical sequencing decision.
